Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd usually wait until I felt like it or when something was coming due. Being 27 and juggling other stuff, I couldn't stick to a strict schedule. I'd listen to lectures while doing other things around the house or during commutes. Some weeks I'd put in solid hours, other weeks barely anything. I know 19 hours sounds decent but it was spread out in random bursts.
Q2: It varied a lot depending on what else was going on in my life. Deadlines were definitely the main driver - if something was due, I'd cram. Otherwise, I'd drift and let days pass without touching course material. I tried setting regular times early on but that fell apart pretty quick. My pattern was more reactive than proactive, responding to pressure rather than planning ahead.
Q3: Usually a deadline reminder or seeing a notification on my phone would prompt me to log in. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything urgent I was missing. The gamified tasks actually got me logging in more because there was that element of earning points or badges. But I'd be lying if I said I logged in regularly out of genuine curiosity - it was mostly obligation or the game elements pulling me back.
Q4: For a brief period when they introduced those gamified tasks, checking the LMS became almost like checking social media. I wanted to see my progress and compete a little. But that wore off after a few weeks. It never really became a stable habit - more like bursts of engagement followed by periods where I'd forget the platform existed. The routine never stuck long-term.
Q5: The video lectures were gold for me since I learn best by listening. I'd play them at 1.5x speed and actually retain more than reading. The discussion forums were surprisingly helpful too - seeing other students' questions made me realize I wasn't alone in being confused. The gamified elements made completing certain tasks feel rewarding even when the content itself didn't excite me.
Q6: The dense reading materials and PDFs I mostly ignored or skimmed at best. My eyes glaze over with walls of text. Some of the supplementary resources felt like busywork, so I'd skip those entirely. If something wasn't directly tied to an assignment or exam, it went to the bottom of my priority list. I know that probably hurt my exam scores but I couldn't bring myself to engage with everything.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it had clear payoff - either points toward my grade or skills I could immediately apply. The gamified tasks worked because there was instant feedback and recognition. If something felt abstract or disconnected from any tangible outcome, I'd question why I was doing it. I needed to see the purpose quickly or I'd lose interest.
Q8: Optional activities felt like suggestions I could safely ignore, honestly. Required ones had weight because they affected my grade. Even when optional stuff seemed interesting, I'd tell myself I'd get to it later and never did. The gamified optional tasks were different though - the game mechanics made them feel less optional because I wanted the rewards. But pure optional readings? Those didn't stand a chance.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a ton of audio lectures and listened to them offline while exercising or cooking. I had notes in a physical notebook that I'd review without any digital trace. Sometimes I'd discuss concepts with a friend who took the course before - none of that shows up anywhere. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the screen.
Q10: There were times I'd spend an hour with my notebook working through problems based on what I'd already downloaded, and the LMS would show nothing. Or I'd be listening to a lecture I'd saved to my phone while on a walk. My peer interactions number is low but I had plenty of conversations about course material in person that weren't tracked. The system only sees a fraction of what I actually did.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once online, download the audio, and then listen to it multiple times offline. Same with slides - I'd view them once in the LMS, screenshot the important ones, and study from my photos. It was more convenient than logging in repeatedly. My one login per week doesn't reflect how much I was actually reviewing materials on my own terms.
Q12: Around midterms when work got hectic and I had personal stuff going on, I just couldn't focus on the course. The content was piling up and I felt behind, which made it harder to even start. There was a period where I'd log in, feel overwhelmed by how much I'd missed, and just close the browser. The gap between where I was and where I should be felt too big to bridge.
Q13: Definitely. When I was tired from work, I'd log in but just stare at the screen without absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities meant the course often lost the battle for my attention. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules entirely rather than face the frustration. My medium stress level was real - I felt the pressure but couldn't always convert it into productive action.
Q14: Yeah, plenty of times. I'd have a video playing while scrolling my phone, technically watching but not really present. Or I'd click through readings quickly just to mark them complete without actually processing the content. My task completion rate looks decent but the quality of that engagement was sometimes pretty shallow. Going through the motions isn't the same as learning.
Q15: I postponed the harder assignments constantly, even knowing they mattered. The exam prep especially - I kept telling myself I'd start tomorrow. Part of it was avoiding the discomfort of struggling with difficult material. Part of it was just being easily distracted by more immediately rewarding things. My resilience helped me bounce back eventually, but I wasted a lot of time in avoidance mode first.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd be inactive for days then suddenly cram everything in the 48 hours before something was due. The LMS would show these spikes of activity right before due dates. It's not healthy but it's how I operated. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have engaged at all. The gamified tasks helped a bit because they created mini-deadlines with rewards.
Q17: When I got good feedback, it motivated me to engage more with similar activities. But feedback often came too late to really change my approach for that assignment. The instant feedback from gamified tasks was more effective - I could adjust immediately. Waiting days for feedback on written work made it feel disconnected from the effort I'd put in.
Q18: The course structure with its 20 online modules felt overwhelming at times. When things were broken into smaller chunks with clear progression, I engaged more. But when it felt like an endless list of requirements, I'd cherry-pick what seemed most important and ignore the rest. The gamification elements added structure that helped me stay somewhat on track even when I was drifting.
Q19: The gamified tasks definitely made engagement easier - they gave me short-term goals and rewards that my brain responded to. What made it harder was the sheer volume of content and the lack of clear prioritization. Sometimes I couldn't tell what was essential versus nice-to-have. Better signposting of critical content would have helped me focus my limited energy more effectively.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for what they need, clicking around trying to understand requirements, or just feeling lost and browsing without direction. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Someone could be confused and repeatedly reviewing the same material without making progress. Or they're engaged with the platform but struggling to convert that into completed work.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat. I did a lot of learning offline that wouldn't show in my activity logs. Someone with low LMS activity might have efficient study habits - they get what they need quickly and study elsewhere. Or they have external resources and support systems. My exam score wasn't great, but my assignment scores were decent partly because I worked on them outside the system.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns - when students are active, what they click on, completion rates. They're good at tracking behavior. But they miss the quality of engagement, the thinking happening offline, the emotional state during study sessions. They can't tell if someone is genuinely learning or just going through motions. My happy mood and high resilience aren't visible in click data.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting mental effort into understanding it. It's not just showing up or clicking through - it's being present and curious. For me, engagement happened in bursts when something clicked or when the gamification made it fun. True engagement is when you think about course concepts even when you're not studying.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I'm actually happy and resilient despite my poor performance. It wouldn't capture my offline listening and studying, my conversations with friends about the material, or the genuine interest I had in some topics even when I didn't complete everything. It also can't show that I'm 27 with a complicated life - my disengagement wasn't about not caring, it was about competing priorities and poor time management.
